rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF The MOMENT LEGACY TRUST(JJM) DATED 12/18/2012, AS Amended TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For Purposes Of Posting The Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4] Per Movant's declaration in support of the Motion, she was a resident of Illinois when she accepted the position of Trustee, and has administered the Trust from her home in Illinois from that time until her resignation as Trustee under Article Eight, paragraph 4 of the Trust Agreement, on January 7, 2026 (Jones Decl. at pars 4, 5, 6, 7, 8). As to the first prong of the three-part test for specific personal jurisdiction, the court finds Petitioner has established by a preponderance of the evidence, presented in the underlying verified petition and verified supplement to petition, that Movant, Linda Rinkema Jones, purposely availed herself of the benefits of this forum vis-à-vis this Trust and her position as its Trustee from its establishment on December 18, 2012 through her resignation in January 2026. As discussed above, the instant Trust was established and executed in California, under California law. The Irrevocable Trust Agreement was signed by the trustors and by Ms. Jones, as Trustee, in California. The trustors at the time of execution were married residents of California, who funded the Trust with their community property membership interest in a California limited liability corporation, which Ms. Jones accepted as Trustee. The sole beneficiaries of the Trust, to whom Ms. Jones undertook fiduciary duties, as a trustee, are minor residents living at all relevant times in San Francisco, California. The court agrees with Movant that Petitioner has not shown by a preponderance of the evidence that the Trust has been administered in San Francisco as stated in the verified Petition. Accordingly, Probate Coe section 17003(a) itself does not confer jurisdiction. The second prong of the three-part test is relatedness; whether there is a substantial connection between Movant's forum activities and the Petition's claims. (Vons Companies, Inc. v. Seabest Foods, Inc. (1996) 14 Cal. 4th 434, 452, 456.) The court finds there is a substantial connection between Ms. Jones' alleged activity in this forum, in her capacity as Trustee, and the verified Petition's allegations of her breach of fiduciary duty in that capacity. The claims and causes of action pleaded in the verified Petition and verified First Supplement to Petition center on Movant's alleged actions as Trustee of the Trust, in breach of her duties to the California beneficiaries and, if proven, causing harm to California residents. As the court has found Petitioner has carried his burden to establish facts justifying the exercise of specific personal jurisdiction over Movant in relation to the verified Petition, the third and final prong of the three-part test shifts the evidentiary burden to Movant, and asks whether the court exercising jurisdiction over her in this trust matter would be fair? To defeat jurisdiction, the defendant must present a compelling case that exercising jurisdiction would be unreasonable. (Burger King, supra, 471 U.S. at p. 477; Vons, supra, 14 Cal.4th at p. 476.) The court does not find Movant has met her burden of showing that this court exercising specific personal jurisdiction over her as to this trust matter would be unreasonable or unfair. Petitioner shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling, as published above.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF The MOMENT LEGACY TRUST(JJM) DATED 12/18/2012, AS Amended TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For Purposes Of Posting The Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4] The first prong of the three-part specific personal jurisdiction test is "purposeful availment." Defendants purposefully avail themselves of a forum's benefits if they intentionally direct their activities at a forum such that, by virtue of the benefit the defendants receive, they should reasonably expect to be subject to jurisdiction there. (Burger King Corp. v. Rudzewicz (1985) 471 U.S. 462, 475-476.) This requirement ensures defendants will not be hailed into a jurisdiction solely because of fortuitous or attenuated contacts or because of the unilateral activity of another party. (Id. at p. 475.) Per the verified Petition, on December 18, 2012, Petitioner Jason Moment and his then-spouse Jessica Moment, as trustors, and Movant, Linda Rinkema Jones, as trustee, executed an Irrevocable Trust Agreement that established The Moment Legacy Trust, an irrevocable trust naming the trustors' children as beneficiaries (attached as Ex. A to Petition). The Irrevocable Trust Agreement provides at Article Eight, subsection 1, that Linda Rinkema Jones "shall be the Trustee of all trusts under this agreement." (Ex. A to Petition.) Per the face of the document, at Article Twelve, subsection 1: "Applicable Law. The trust herein provided for has been created in the State of California and accepted by the Trustee therein, and all questions pertaining to validity, interpretation and administration thereof shall be determined in accordance with the law of the State of California." The Trust document was notarized as to each signatory in Marin County, California (Ex. A to Petition). Schedule A of the Trust was signed "accepted" by Movant, Linda Rinkema Jones, as Trustee (Ex. A to Petition). The sole asset listed on the Schedule A is a partial membership interest in a California limited liability company. (Ex. A to Petition). Per the verified Petition and verified First Supplement to Petition, the Trust was initially funded with a 100% Membership Interest in a California limited liability company, which was in turn invested in an investment fund founded and managed by Petitioner, as part of Route One Investment Company, L.P. Per the verified First Supplement to Petition, the trustors were married and both residents of California at the time the Trust was established; the assets listed on the Schedule A were at the time of transfer were held by the trustors as community property; the Trust beneficiaries, the trustors' two children, are life-long residents of San Francisco, California (FSP at 6:11-16). Per the verified Petition, following the couple's 2023 dissolution of marriage judgment entered in San Francisco County (Ex. C of Petition), Movant, as trustee, executed a document titled The Moment Legacy Trust Dated December 18, 2012 Division Instrument, on December 30, 2024. Per the face of that document, "Now, therefore, in accordance with the foregoing, Linda Rinkema Jones, as Trustee of the Trust, hereby exercises the power set forth in Article Seven, paragraph 2, subparagraph (1) of the Trust Agreement to divide the Trust into two separate, equal sub-trusts, each sub-trust of which will own a fifty-percent (50%) membership in the Company and shall be governed by the provision of the Trust Agreement and one sub-trust shall be referred to as "THE MOMENT LEGACY TRUST (JEM)" and the other sub-trust shall be referred to as "THE MOMENT LEGACY TRUST (JJM)."" (Ex. C of Petition.) Per the verified Petition, Movant remained Trustee of the JJM Trust subtrust, associated with her daughter Jessica Moment. (Pet. at 2:21-25.) The verified Petition is captioned as to The Moment Legacy Trust (JJM) subtrust.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF The MOMENT LEGACY TRUST(JJM) DATED 12/18/2012, AS Amended TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Specially Appearing Respondent Linda Jones To Quash For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Michael B. McNaughton. Before the court is Motion of Specially Appearing Respondent Linda Jones to Quash for Lack of Personal Jurisdiction ("Motion"). The Motion is DENIED. California appellate law most recently discussed case-specific personal jurisdiction in the context of a California trust in Van Buskirk v. Van Buskirk (2020) 53 Cal. App. 5th 523. As discussed in Van Buskirk, As matters of state law, personal jurisdiction rules are the same for civil and trust proceedings. (See Prob. Code, [sec.] 17004 ["The court may exercise jurisdiction in proceedings under this division on any basis permitted by Section 410.10 of the Code of Civil Procedure."].) California courts may exercise jurisdiction to determine matters concerning trust property located in California-particularly land-even if the trust is administered elsewhere. (Cal. Law Revision Com. com., 54A pt. 1 West's Ann. Prob. Code (2011 ed.) foll. [sec.] 17004, p. 306.) Also as a matter of state law, California courts may exercise jurisdiction on any basis consistent with the state or federal Constitutions. (Code Civ. Proc., [sec.] 410.10.) Van Buskirk, supra, 53 Cal. App. 5th at 530-31. Jurisdiction is proper if a defendant has minimum contacts with the state such that the lawsuit does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice. (Daimler AG v. Bauman (2014) 571 U.S. 117, 126.) Personal jurisdiction can be "general" or "specific." With specific, case-linked jurisdiction, the court may adjudicate only those issues deriving from, or connected with, the very controversy that establishes jurisdiction. (Bristol-Myers Squibb Co. v. Superior Ct. of California, San Francisco Cnty. (2017) 582 U.S. 255, 262-264.) Van Buskirk summarized the three-part test of when specific jurisdiction is proper: (1) defendants have purposefully availed themselves of forum benefits; (2) the controversy relates to the defendants' contacts with the forum; and (3) the exercise of jurisdiction comports with fair play and substantial justice. Van Buskirk, supra, 53 Cal. App. 5th at 531, quoting Pavlovich v. Superior Court (2002) 29 Cal.4th 262, 269. When a defendant moves to quash service for lack of personal jurisdiction, the plaintiff must establish by a preponderance of the evidence the facts justifying the exercise of jurisdiction. The burden then shifts to the defendant to demonstrate exercising jurisdiction would be unreasonable. "Jurisdictional facts must be proved by admissible evidence. This generally requires declarations by competent witnesses. A properly verified complaint may be treated as a declaration for this purpose." (Van Buskirk 53 Cal. App. 5th at 530, 535, quoting Jayone Foods, Inc. v. Aekyung Industrial Co. Ltd. (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 543, 553 and Weil & Brown, Cal. Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2020) [par.] 3:387.) The underlying Petition is verified. The First Supplement to Petition is verified. The Motion is verified, and supported by a declaration by Movant, signed under penalty of perjury under the laws of California. The Response Addressing Issues Raised by Examiner, filed by Movant on May 4, 2026, after the Motion was filed on April 7, 2026, is verified.
